macro_segments.title: Introduction and Overview of the Daf: Collateral, Possession, and Chametz over Pesach

micro_segments.title: Welcome and framing of today's sugya: standalone legal unit on creditors, debtors, and collateral
Scope of material: from Lamed (30) Amud Bet to Lamed Aleph (31) Amud Bet, examining when collateral is in creditor's vs. debtor's possession and its impact on chametz she'avar alav ha'Pesach
The Mishnah on Pesachim 30b: Non-Jew loans to Jew on chametz collateral—permitted to derive benefit after Pesach; Jew loans to Non-Jew on chametz—prohibited

macro_segments.title: The Amoraic Debate: Abaye vs. Rava on When Creditor Takes Possession of Collateral

micro_segments.title: Itmara: Abaye holds creditor acquires retroactively (lemafrea); Rava holds creditor acquires prospectively (mikan u'lehaba)
Clarification of when the dispute applies: both agree when borrower uses collateral; dispute concerns when lender uses or sells collateral
Abaye's reasoning: retroactively revealed that creditor had claim from inception; Rava's reasoning: creditor acquires only upon default when repayment becomes impossible

macro_segments.title: Case of Reuven, Shimon, and the Field: Applying the Debate to Inheritance and Debt Chains

micro_segments.title: Reuven sells field to Shimon with achrayut (warranty that Reuven will repay if Reuven's creditor claims the field); Shimon also owes Reuven the purchase price via promissory note
Reuven dies; Reuven's creditor comes to claim the field from Shimon; Shimon pays off creditor; then Reuven's heirs claim Shimon still owes the original debt
Rava's suggestion: Shimon should give field to heirs for the debt, then reclaim it via achrayut to pay creditor, avoiding double payment
Problem: How can Rava (mikan u'lehaba) justify creditor collecting from heirs if heirs acquired the field themselves?
Resolution: The specific stipulation that the lender said 'just as I am obligated to you, so too to the creditor,' following Rabbi Natan's principle of debt chain passing

macro_segments.title: Connecting Amoraic Debate to the Mishnah: How the Mishnah's Cases Accord with Abaye and Rava

micro_segments.title: Question: If Rava holds creditor acquires only after default, how can Mishnah say chametz is mutar b'hana'ah (permitted) after Pesach when it was in Jew's reshut during Pesach?
Resolution: The case must be one where the Jew deposited the chametz with the Non-Jew (keshehirhino etzlo), making it sufficiently the Non-Jew's possession even under Rava's view

macro_segments.title: Tannaic Parallels: Rabbi Meir and Tanna Kamma on Who Acquires Collateral in Jew-Jew vs. Jew-Nokhri Transactions

micro_segments.title: Braita: Jew lends to Non-Jew on chametz collateral—Tanna Kamma: Jew does not transgress; Rabbi Meir: Jew does transgress
Can we explain the Tannaic dispute via Abaye/Rava debate (retroactive vs. prospective acquisition)? Problem: the two cases are not mirror-parallel
Braita continued: Non-Jew lends to Jew on chametz collateral—all agree Jew transgresses chametz she'avar alav ha'Pesach

macro_segments.title: Rabbi Yitzchak's Principle: Creditor Acquires Collateral Only in Jew-Jew Loans; Impact on Nokhri Cases

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Yitzchak derives from Deuteronomy 24 (return of collateral at night) that creditor must own the collateral—otherwise returning it would not be 'tzedakah'
Tanna Kamma: principle of ba'al chov koneh mashkon applies only to Jew-Jew loans (where tzedakah mitzvah exists); not Jew-Nokhri (no mitzvah of tzedakah to non-Jew)
Rabbi Meir: applies kal vachomer—if Jew-Jew acquires, then Jew-Nokhri all the more so acquires
Implication for Mishnah's Braita: When Nokhri lends to Jew, even if deposited, Nokhri mi-Yisrael never acquires collateral; therefore Jew always violates chametz she'avar

macro_segments.title: Resolving Apparent Contradiction: Me'achshav (Explicit Stipulation) vs. Without Stipulation

micro_segments.title: New okimta: Mishnah's case (where Non-Jew loans Jew on chametz and it's mutar) must be where Non-Jew said to Jew 'me'achshav' (from now this is yours)
Braita: Non-Jew deposits fancy loaf (patporni) with Jew—no transgression; but if Non-Jew says 'higaticha' (it has arrived in your possession), then Jew transgresses
Inference: The difference between the two clauses of the braita demonstrates that explicit stipulation (me'achshav / higaticha) changes the status of collateral ownership

macro_segments.title: Practical Application: Presumptions of Ownership in Stores Owned by Jews vs. Non-Jews

micro_segments.title: Braita: Jewish-owned store with Jewish inventory, but Non-Jewish workers enter—chametz found after Pesach is assur b'hana'ah (presumed to be Jewish owner's chametz)
Braita: Non-Jewish-owned store with Non-Jewish inventory, but Jewish workers enter—chametz found after Pesach is mutar b'achila (presumed to be Non-Jew owner's chametz)

topical_tags.term: mikan u'lehaba hu goveh (creditor acquires prospectively)
ba'al chov koneh mashkon (creditor acquires collateral)
chametz she'avar alav ha'Pesach (chametz over which Pesach passed)
bal yera'eh u'bal yimatze (not seen, not found)
achrayut (warranty; seller's obligation to repay if creditor claims collateral)
me'achshav (from now; explicit stipulation of transfer)
patporni (fancy or important loaf of bread)
higaticha (it has arrived in your possession)
reshut (domain in context of chametz ownership)
